runs.interpretation: Global labor-supply substitution-response elasticity override used in a U.S. microsimulation model.
citations: Chetty (2012) on bounded labor supply elasticities / frictions
Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) on taxable income and behavioral responses
Keane (2011) review of labor supply elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: My central belief for a uniform U.S. microsimulation override is a modest positive substitution elasticity around 0.2, reflecting common calibration choices for prime-age labor supply under tax changes. I allow lower values near 0.1 and upper values into the 0.3–0.6 range to cover heterogeneity across groups and specification differences, but very large elasticities seem less plausible for a single global parameter.

Quantity of interest:
- Name: Substitution elasticity of labor supply in a tax-benefit simulation
- Definition: Response coefficient multiplying the bounded relative change in the effective marginal wage in a U.S. tax-benefit simulation. A 1 percent change in the effective marginal wage changes labor supply by this fraction. If set, this version applies uniformly across individuals.
- Target interpretation: Global labor-supply substitution-response elasticity override used in a U.S. microsimulation model
- Population/context: Adults in the United States under a current-law tax-benefit simulation
- Units: elasticity

runs.interpretation: Global labor-supply substitution-response elasticity override used in a U.S. microsimulation model.
citations: Chetty (2012) on bounds/frictions and taxable income/labor supply responses
Eissa & Liebman (1996) on labor supply responses of single mothers to the EITC
Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) on elasticity concepts in tax-response modeling
runs.reasoningSummary: For a single uniform substitution elasticity used as a global override in a U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation, I would center on a modest positive intensive-margin response. My median is 0.2: large enough to allow behavioral adjustment, but below classic higher elasticities because aggregate adult labor supply, especially for prime-age workers, is often estimated as fairly inelastic. The upper tail allows stronger responses for secondary earners/extensive-margin effects, while the lower tail reflects near-zero intensive responses.

runs.reasoningSummary: My recalled prior is that a uniform uncompensated/behavioral labor-supply response used in tax microsimulation is usually modest, with intensive-margin substitution elasticities often around 0.1–0.3 for prime-age adults, higher for some groups. For a single global override in a U.S. model, 0.2 is my best central value, with substantial uncertainty and an upper tail allowing more responsive specifications.

For a single global override in a U.S. model, 0.2 is my best central value, with substantial uncertainty and an upper tail allowing more responsive specifications."}

- Target interpretation: Global labor-supply substitution-response elasticity override used in a U.S. microsimulation model

- Units: elasticity
